feed.title: SAP Community - ABAP Development
feed.subtitle: ABAP Development blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: How to extend the program SAPF124 of F.13 to retrieve its results
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • Selection screen parameters and result example
  • SAPF124 logic
  • Subroutine ECHTLAUF with I_KOART being 'D', 'K' or 'S'
  • Subroutine SPERREN_KONTO (lock)
  • Subroutine AUSGLEICH with I_SUM = sum of amounts of all documents with the same clearing criteria
  • Subroutine ACC_APPEND_DATA
  • Enhancements
  • Basic code to run SAPF124
  • ABAP type for the collected data
  • SQL showing a subset of all possible test data

Introduction

F.13 is the transaction to clear the open items automatically. For functional information, see Automatic Clearing | SAP Help Portal.

The result is a spool which contains a log with all results.

To analyze the results by program, it's easier to create a few enhancements to F.13 (program SAPF124) to retrieve the log as several internal tables, rather than decoding the spool (called "ABAP List" hereafter), because the latter is often subject to changes after an upgrade.

Note that some people recommend in the forum to use POSTING_INTERFACE_CLEARING but I couldn't find any official information saying that it's allowed for automating F.13.

Before creating the enhancements to the program SAPF124, it's important to understand the logic of the program. Also, the names of some of the selection screen parameters and an example of the output ABAP List are useful.

Warning: in this blog post, I don't post the code I used, but I explain which subroutines can be enhanced.

My SAP system is S/4HANA 2023 FPS01.

 

Selection screen parameters and result example

Example of the output ABAP List. Here F.13 ran in test mode so there's no clearing document created (so, the number remains blank). We can see the list of cleared documents at the top, then the additional log and the error log at the bottom.

 

SAPF124 logic

AT SELECTION-SCREEN:

  • Call the subroutine CHECK_ECHTL
    • In productive mode (X_TESTL not checked), it displays the warning FG013 “This program run is a production run” as a popup.
    • It also calculates X_ECHTL = “X” or “ ”.
      • “X” means that the execution is in productive mode (X_TESTL not checked).

 

START-OF-SELECTION:

  • Call the subroutine KONTOTABELLEN_FUELLEN
    • Fills the 3 lists of objects to process: customers (from the table BSID to the internal table TDEBI), suppliers (from the table BSIK to the internal table TKREDI) and accounts (from the table SKB1 to the internal table TSAKO), according to the selection screen parameters.
  • Logic for the 3 lists
    • Call the subroutine DEBI_VERARBEITEN if the "Customers" box is checked
      • LOOP AT TDEBI (customers)
        • Call the subroutine ECHTLAUF or TESTLAUF (*) with KOART = 'D'
    • Call the subroutine KREDI_VERARBEITEN if the "Suppliers" box is checked
      • LOOP AT TKREDI (suppliers)
        • Call the subroutine ECHTLAUF or TESTLAUF with KOART = 'K'
          • (ECHTLAUF or TESTLAUF based on the same test as for customers)
    • Call the subroutine SAKO_VERARBEITEN if the "Accounts" box is checked
      • LOOP AT TSAKO (accounts)
        • Call the subroutine ECHTLAUF or TESTLAUF with KOART = 'S'
          • (ECHTLAUF or TESTLAUF based on the same test as for customers)

(*) ECHTLAUF is called if the run is in productive mode or with tolerance included. Otherwise TESTLAUF is called, which has the same logic as ECHTLAUF except that the lock isn't done.

 

Event END-OF-SELECTION:

  • Call the subroutine ACC_AUSGABE_ADDITION_LOG
    • Write the internal table IPROT (additional log) to the ABAP List.
  • IF X_FEHLER = 'X':
    • Call the subroutine ACC_AUSGABE_FAULT_LOG
      • It writes the internal table FEHLPROT (error messages) to the ABAP List.

 

Subroutine ECHTLAUF with I_KOART being 'D', 'K' or 'S'

For information, depending on I_KOART being 'D', 'K' or 'S', the data will be taken respectively from TDEBI, TKREDI or TSAKO.

Reminder: ECHTLAUF is called only if the run is in productive mode or with tolerance included. Otherwise TESTLAUF is called, which has the same logic as ECHTLAUF except that the lock isn't done (SPERREN_KONTO is not called).

Logic:

  • Call the subroutine SPERREN_KONTO to lock the object to process
  • Call the subroutine INDEXTABELLEN_FUELLEN
    • Call the subroutine READ_BSID or READ_BSIK or READ_BSIS to build the groups of clearing criteria XBSIDGR or XBSIKGR or XBSISGR, depending on I_KOART = 'D', 'K' or 'S' respectively. The criteria (list of fields) are defined in the SAP Customizing Implementation Guide (SPRO) -> Financial Accounting -> General Ledger Accounting -> Business Transactions -> Open Item Clearing -> Prepare Automatic Clearing (or SM30 V_TF123). The subroutine XBSID_XBSIDGR_FUELLEN or XBSIK_XBSIKGR_FUELLEN or XBSIS_XBSISGR_FUELLEN is called, which fills these internal tables:
      • APPEND XBSID or XBSIK or XBSIS depending on I_KOART = 'D', 'K' or 'S' respectively.
      • COLLECT XBSIDGR or XBSIKGR or XBSISGR depending on I_KOART = 'D', 'K' or 'S' respectively:
        • Each line is a group of clearing criteria for which FI documents may be cleared altogether.
        • The field XBSIDGR-BET_BW or XBSIKGR-BET_BW or XBSISGR-BET_TW contains the sum of FI documents which can be zero (they can be cleared) or another number (they cannot be cleared).
    • LOOP AT XBSIDGR or XBSIKGR or XBSISGR depending on I_KOART = 'D', 'K' or 'S' respectively
      • Call subroutine AUSGLEICH USING I_SUM = XBSIDGR-BET_BW or XBSIKGR-BET_BW or XBSISGR-BET_TW

 

Subroutine SPERREN_KONTO (lock)

  • If KOART = 'D', call ENQUEUE_EFKNB1A with tdebi-bukrs, tdebi-kunnr.
  • If KOART = 'K', call ENQUEUE_EFLFB1A with tkredi-bukrs, tkredi-lifnr.
  • If KOART = 'S', call ENQUEUE_EFSKB1A with tsako-bukrs, tsako-hkont, tsako-hbkid, tsako-hktid.

 

Subroutine AUSGLEICH with I_SUM = sum of amounts of all documents with the same clearing criteria

It checks if the clearing of one group of documents with the same clearing criteria is possible. If yes, it’s calling a given transaction code (whose code may vary) via Batch Input to create one clearing document.

The subroutine has the following logic:

  • Call the subroutine BELEGE_AUSGEBEN (collect the list of documents to be cleared)
    • Loop at all the lines of XBSID, XBSIK or XBSIS corresponding to the current grouping criteria XBSIDGR, XBSIKGR or XBSISGR depending on KOART = 'D', 'K' or 'S' respectively.
      • Call the subroutine RECORD_DOCUMENT
        • APPEND GS_DLIST TO GT_DLIST. It's the list of FI documents corresponding to the current clearing criteria group.
  • If I_SUM = 0 (the documents of the current group have a total amount of 0, so they can be cleared).
    • Call the subroutine AUGDT_PRUEFEN
      • Call the subroutine CALL_TRANSACTION if it's a productive run
        • Batch input CALL TRANSACTION FB1D, FB1K or FB1S/FB1SL depending on KOART = 'D', 'K' or 'S' respectively.
        • The batch input messages are stored into the internal table MESSTAB
        • Call the subroutine MESSTAB_AUSWERTEN
          • If MESSTAB contains the success message F5312 "Document & was posted in company code &", AUGBLNR (global variable) = MSGV1 (document number)
          • Call the subroutine RECORD_CLEARING_INFO with I_CHAR = 'AUGBL'
            • Call subroutine ACC_APPEND_DATA with parameters AUGBLNR, clearing date and ID_MESSAGE = ‘AUGBL’ to display the documents to be cleared and the clearing document created.
            • IMPORTANT: GT_DLIST is cleared (the list of documents to be cleared). 
              • Call subroutine RECORD_CLEARING_INFO with I_CHAR = code expressing errors (‘NOCLEAR’, ‘NOTZERO’, etc.)
          • If X_FEHLER = 'X'.
            • Call subroutine MESSTAB_LESEN which feeds the internal table FEHLPROT (displayed error log) from MESSTAB (error messages from the batch input)
  • Else if clearing is permitted with tolerance
    • TODO
  • Else (if no clearing)
    • Call subroutine ACC_APPEND_DATA with parameter ID_MESSAGE = ‘NOTZERO’.
    • If there’s some clearing tolerance, add the error to the internal table IPROT (additional log).

 

Subroutine ACC_APPEND_DATA

It receives the parameters:

  • ID_AUGBLNR = clearing document created
  • ID_AUGDT = clearing date
  • ID_MESSAGE = ‘AUGBL’ (clearing) or others (no clearing), etc.
    • AUGBL: The clearing has been done, i.e. the clearing document has been created, the global variable AUGBL contains this clearing document, the log contains F5312 (*).
    • NOTZERO: the clearing is not possible because the sum of amounts of the documents is not zero.
    • AUGDT: Test mode successful (in test mode, the F5312 and F5263 (*) messages are absent and there is no error).
    • NOCLEAR: The reconciliation document has failed (the F5312 (*) message is absent and no tolerance is requested or a tolerance is requested but F5263 is absent).
    • NOCLEARTOL: Failed (the F5312 (*) message is absent and a tolerance is requested and F5263 is present).
    • ENQ: failed because the object of the current clearing criteria could not be locked
    • Other values: Failed
    • (*) Batch Input may return one of the following messages:
      • F5312: "Document & was posted in company code &"
      • F5263: "The difference is too large for clearing"

This subroutine has the following logic:

  • Call the subroutine WRITE_LISTE if the output of cleared/clearing documents has been requested in the selection screen. This subroutine does:
    • LOOP AT GT_DLIST INTO GS_DLIST (list of documents to be cleared)
      • The document is written to the ABAP List.
    • Call subroutine CHECK_SUMME
      • The clearing criteria are written to the ABAP List on one line, including the new clearing document if one has been created.
  • GT_DLIST is emptied (the list of documents to be cleared).

 

Enhancements

The parameter X_FEHLER (to display error messages in the ABAP List) is absolutely necessary to run several SAPF124 subroutines which contain information about the cleared documents.

The following subroutines may be enhanced to obtain information about the cleared documents used and the clearing documents created:

  • Begin of CHECK_ECHTL
    • In real mode, it displays the warning FG013 “This program run is a production run”.
    • It also calculates X_ECHTL = “X” or “ ”. “X” means that the execution is in real mode (case F.13 is not called in test mode i.e., checkbox X_TESTL = space/false).
    • This message is annoying if SAPF124 is called by a custom program in a loop.
    • Do this enhancement:
      • Keep calculating X_ECHTL = “X” or “ ” but don’t display the warning by using ABAP “RETURN” to not execute the standard code of the subroutine.
  • Begin of ACC_APPEND_DATA
    • It’s called after the logic to create one clearing document, whatever it has succeeded (parameter ID_MESSAGE = ‘AUGBL’) or failed (other values).
    • Do this enhancement:
      • Collect the list of documents cleared (internal table GT_DLIST), the clearing status (ID_MESSAGE) and the clearing document created (ID_AUGBLNR). The clearing document will be empty if the creation failed.
  • Begin of MESSTAB_LESEN
    • It’s called when the creation of the cleared document failed via Batch Input and it feeds the internal table FEHLPROT from MESSTAB (error messages from Batch Input).
    • Do this enhancement:
      • Collect the list of documents cleared (internal table GT_DLIST) and the error messages from the internal table FEHLPROT.
  • Begin of ACC_AUSGABE_FAULT_LOG
    • It’s called at the end, but only if the selection screen option X_FEHLER was selected.
    • Do this enhancement:
      • Export the data previously collected by the enhancements to the ABAP memory (EXPORT TO MEMORY ID).

 

Basic code to run SAPF124

Transaction F.13 starts SAPF124 with the SAP&F124 variant, so I'm doing the same thing. I haven't checked to see if there's a difference without the variant.

The actual content is obtained by reading the data collected by the enhancements in the ABAP memory (IMPORT FROM MEMORY ID).

SUBMIT sapf124
    USING SELECTION-SET 'SAP&F124'
    WITH bukrx   IN ir_bukrs     " Company
    WITH gjahx   IN ir_gjahr     " Year
    WITH docnr   IN ir_belnr     " Document Number
    WITH x_kunnr  = iv_xkunnr    " Check "Customers"
    WITH kontd   IN ir_kunnr     " Customers
    WITH x_lifnr  = iv_xlifnr    " Check "Suppliers"
    WITH kontk   IN ir_lifnr     " Suppliers
    WITH x_saknr  = iv_xsaknr    " Check "Accounts"
    WITH konts   IN ir_saknr     " Accounts
    WITH x_testl  = iv_xtestl    " Test mode
    WITH xausbel  = abap_true    " Output the documents cleared
    WITH xnausbel = abap_true    " Output the documents not cleared
    WITH x_fehler = abap_true    " Output the error messages
    EXPORTING LIST TO MEMORY     " To not generate a spool
    AND RETURN.

 

ABAP type for the collected data

In my final code, a variable of type TT_CLEARING_RESULT is exported by the enhancement to the ABAP memory, and imported after SUBMIT SAPF124, which is defined as follows:

    TYPES:
      BEGIN OF ts_doc_item_to_be_cleared,
        bukrs TYPE bseg-bukrs,
        belnr TYPE bseg-belnr,
        gjahr TYPE bseg-gjahr,
        buzei TYPE bseg-buzei,
      END OF ts_doc_item_to_be_cleared.
    TYPES tt_doc_item_to_be_cleared TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ts_doc_item_to_be_cleared WITH DEFAULT KEY.
    TYPES:
      BEGIN OF ts_clearing_doc_created,
        bukrs TYPE bseg-bukrs,
        belnr TYPE bseg-belnr,
        gjahr TYPE bseg-gjahr,
      END OF ty_ clearing_doc_created.
    "! Batch Input errors
    TYPES tt_journal_sapf124 TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF bdcmsgcoll WITH DEFAULT KEY.
    TYPES:
      BEGIN OF ts_clearing_result
        s_clearing_doc_created   TYPE ts_clearing_doc_created
        t_doc_item_to_be_cleared TYPE tt_doc_item_to_be_cleared,
        t_journal                TYPE tt_journal_sapf124,
      END OF ts_clearing_result.
    TYPES tt_clearing_result TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ts_clearing_result WITH DEFAULT KEY.

Depending on the selection criteria when calling SAPF124, the result may contain many rows, some failing, others succeeding. For example, to clear 2 documents A 2024 and B 2025, which we know can be cleared, unfortunately the selection will be wide and there may be 4 documents selected: A 2024, A 2025, B 2024, B 2025.

In the result, there will likely be failures on A 2025 and B 2024, and a success for A 2024 and B 2025. Since we are only interested in the 2 documents A 2024 and B 2025, we must search the result for the row concerning them.

Example of collected data of type TT_CLEARING_RESULT :

Only the 6th line shows a success, the clearing document 0900002549 has been created to clear the documents shown in the component T_DOC_ITEM_TO_BE_CLEARED:

 

SQL showing a subset of all possible test data

This query may be useful for developers who don't know FI and want to run F.13 with any existing test data, here it's returning document numbers from suppliers, without withholding tax, which can be cleared; you may need to adjust the clearing criteria according to your V_TF123 settings (in my case, grouping by GSBER, EBELN except for suppliers 6*):

select bsik1.bukrs || '   ' Company, bsik1.lifnr || '  ' Supplier, bsik1.h_waers, bsik1.gsber, bsik1.ebeln || '     ' ebeln, bsik1.belnr || '  ' Document, bsik1.gjahr || '  ' Year, bsik1.shkzg, bsik1.wrbtr
from bseg bsik1, bkpf
where bkpf.mandt = bsik1.mandt
  and bkpf.bukrs = bsik1.bukrs
  and bkpf.belnr = bsik1.belnr
  and bkpf.gjahr = bsik1.gjahr
  and bsik1.mandt = '100'
  and bsik1.umsks = ' '
  and bsik1.xpypr = ' '
  and bkpf.xstov = ' '
  and bsik1.qsshb = 0 /* without withholding tax to simplify */
  and bsik1.koart = 'K'
  and bsik1.augbl = ''
  and bsik1.h_bstat <> 'D'
  and bsik1.h_bstat <> 'M'
  and exists ( select bsik.bukrs, bsik.lifnr, bsik.h_waers, bsik.gsber, bsik.ebeln
from bseg bsik, bkpf
where bkpf.mandt = bsik.mandt
  and bkpf.bukrs = bsik.bukrs
  and bkpf.belnr = bsik.belnr
  and bkpf.gjahr = bsik.gjahr
  and bsik.bukrs = bsik1.bukrs
  and bsik.lifnr = bsik1.lifnr
  and bsik.h_waers = bsik1.h_waers
  and bsik.gsber = bsik1.gsber
  and bsik.ebeln = bsik1.ebeln
  and bsik.umsks = ' '
  and bsik.xpypr = ' '
  and bkpf.xstov = ' '
  and bsik.qsshb = 0 /* without withholding tax to simplify */
  and bsik.koart = 'K'
  and bsik.augbl = ''
  and bsik.h_bstat <> 'D'
  and bsik.h_bstat <> 'M'
  and bsik.lifnr not between '6000000000' and '6999999999'             /* criteria from V_TF123 */
group by bsik.bukrs, bsik.lifnr, bsik.h_waers, bsik.gsber, bsik.ebeln    /* bukrs, lifnr, waers (waers to simplify) + criteria from V_TF123 */
having 0 = sum( case when bsik.shkzg = 'H' then - bsik.wrbtr else + bsik.wrbtr end )
)
order by bsik1.bukrs, bsik1.lifnr, bsik1.h_waers, bsik1.gsber, bsik1.ebeln, bsik1.belnr, bsik1.gjahr

entry.title: Adding Custom Fields to a Fiori App Using Custom Fields and Logic
entry.content:
Introduction: 

SAP S/4 HANA provides in-app extensibility and the Adaptation Transport Organizer (ATO) framework to safely create, enable and transport custom fields without modifying standard code. In this blog, I will walk through a step-by-step process to set up ATO, prepare custom fields, enable them, publish them and finally add then to a custom Fiori app.

Step 1: Initial ATO (Adaptation Transport Organizer) set up:

  1. Go to transaction SCFD_EUI

2. Close the initial pop up and click on Auto Setup and following screen will populate:

3. Create below packages using SE80:

  1. Local package TEST_KEY_USER_LOCAL
  2. Sandbox package TEST_KEY_USER_SANDBOX

4. Now enter the local and sandbox package name and click on ‘Setup with specific data’.

5. It will then prompt you for a transport, for this first create a workbench transport and assign it in the popup to enter the transport and save your entries.

6. Input table name ‘GMGR’ and field ‘Z*’ and hit Execute:

Step 2 – Understanding Field Statuses

After execution the output screen shows three possible field statuses:

Enabled: This is already enabled and available in custom field APP

Can Be Enabled: This can be enabled by clicking Enable button in tool bar

Preparation Required: Click on Prepare button, it will show the further steps to make it

‘CAN BE ENABLED’ status.

Step3: Changing Status from preparation required to can be enabled

  1. Select ZZPIAR_EMAIL and click on Prepare.

2. The message below will be shown:

3. Go to T-code SE11 and check GMGR table.

4. The field ZZPIAR_EMAIL does not have domain associated

5. Once Domain is created for this field, the status of the field will become ‘CAN BE ENABLED’.

Step4: Enabling the custom field

  1. The below field ZZFINALEXP is in ‘CAN BE ENABLED’ status:

2. Select ZZFINALEXP and click Enable button:

3. Select correct enabling type and click ok:

4. Select the package and create a workbench transport and Provide Transport number at Request/Task click ok.

5. We can see that the field ZZFINALEXP status changed to ‘Enabled’ status:

Step5: Publish the custom Field

  1. Go to T-code /n/ui2/flp
  2. Login to FIORI
  3. Execute ‘custom field’ app
  4. The below screen appears, this app shows all ‘Enabled’ fields, we see ‘Final Expense’ field here which we have enabled in above steps(step3).
  5. Now select ‘FINAL EXPENSE’ field and click on the arrow.

6. The screen below opens, now enable Additional selection-Grant

7. Click on publish

The field is published now.

Step6: Add Custom field to ‘Custom’ App

  1. Now go to the custom app and click on ‘Adapt UI’ as shown below.

2. Now the page becomes ‘Editable’, right click on screen, we can see option add field

3. Select Final expense field here and click OK.

4. Final Expense field will be added to the screen, now click Publish and save and exit.

Conclusion:

By following these steps, you can manage custom fields using SAP’s standard extensibility framework without touching the core objects. This approach reduced upgrade risk, supports transport consistency, and aligns with SAP’s clean-core strategy. The same method can be reused for other tables and fields across S/4HANA landscape.

entry.title: How to Deliver Customizing as a SaaS Provider using Business Configuration Sets
entry.content:
In our previous blog post, How to Provide Customizing as a SaaS Provider, we described a programmatic approach that used EML statements with explicitly coded object templates in application jobs to deliver customizing entries. With release 2511, you can now use Business Configuration Sets instead, removing the need to include explicitly coded customizing data in your business logic.

This blog illustrates how to maintain customizing data in a Business Configuration Maintenance Object, how to maintain customizing translations, importing the created customizing data/translations into a BC Set, implementing an application job for the BC Set activation and finally how to deploy the BC Set content to the relevant customizing table.
We will also have a look into how BC Sets can be updated programmatically for the case when the customizing data was changed and BC Set data needs to be reimported.

This blog presents an alternative method to the Fiori app Upload Business Configuration. One restriction of the Upload Business Configuration app is that it does not validate the customizing data being uploaded.
In contrast, customizing data imported into Business Configuration (BC) Sets via ADT originates from existing customizing requests. This data is created within custom business configuration objects, where it is automatically validated through the underlying BO behavior (for example, error codes greater than 500 are rejected):

This blog covers the following topics:

  • Create Business Configuration Maintenance Object
  • Providing Authorization Control for a Business Configuration Maintenance Object
  • Creating customizing data & translations
  • Creating a BC Set and importing customizing data & translations
  • Creating an Application Job for the BC Set activation
  • Deploying the BC Set content by running the application job
  • Maintaining customizing data programmatically in BC Sets

Create Business Configuration Maintenance Object

The Business Configuration Maintenance Object discussed in this blog can be developed using the following tutorial, which guides you in creating a table for storing error codes:

Create Business Configuration Maintenance Object | SAP Tutorials

Providing Authorization Control for a Business Configuration Maintenance Object

To use the Business Configuration Maintenance Object, the required authorization must be set up. How to do this is explained in this tutorial:

Providing Authorization Control for a Business Configuration Maintenance Object | SAP Tutorials

Creating customizing data & translations

Maintain following customizing data as described in tutorial Use Custom Business Configurations app on a new customizing request:

Error Code

Description

400

Bad Request

401

Unauthorized

403

Forbidden

Afterwards your customizing request should include the primary customizing data as Customizing: Table Contents. Such customizing requests can be used for importing into a BC Set. Corresponding data records and translations will be extracted automatically during the process.

Maintain customizing translations for source language English United States and target language, for example German Germany, for the error codes using the Maintain Customizing Translations app.

Select the text table ZERRCODET_### that was created earlier as text source in your translation project.

Creating a BC Set and importing customizing data & translations

Follow the steps provided in Creating Business Configuration Sets to create a new business configuration set and use the customizing request that was created in the previous step for importing the data.

After successful import from the customizing request the error code data records as well as translations are included:

We can now proceed to implement an application job that can be used activate this BC Set.

Note: To import customizing data into a BC Set, the customizing data needs to be maintained for customizing objects of type Individual Transaction Object. A business configuration maintenance object (CUBCO) is configured as such by defining a transport object definition (TOBJ) in the service configuration settings of the CUBCO. By using the business configuration maintenance object wizard as described in Create Business Configuration Maintenance Object | SAP Tutorials, this prerequisite is fulfilled. For more information, please refer to Generating a Business Configuration Maintenance Object with the Generate ABAP Repository Objects Wizard → BC Management. Configuration changes are then recorded with object type TDAT instead of a TABU.

Those business configuration maintenance objects without existing transport object definition must be migrated, and a transport object definition needs to be created manually. How this can be done is described in the blog post How to enable BC Set support for BC app.

Creating an Application Job for the BC Set activation

In this example, an Application Job is used for creating and updating the customizing entries in a SaaS tenant. The customizing entries are deployed within the Application Job's implementation by activating the business configuration set using the corresponding runtime API.

As a first step, implement the BC Set activation as the business logic of the of the application job with a parameter for the BC Set to be activated and while saving activation messages in the application log:

CLASS zcl_errorcode_set_activate_000 DEFINITION
  PUBLIC FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    "! <p class="shorttext synchronized">Error Codes BC Set</p>
    DATA errorcode_bc_set TYPE cl_scpr_cd_bcset_activation=>ty_bcset_id VALUE 'Z_ERROR_CODES_SET_000'.

    INTERFACES if_apj_rt_run.
    INTERFACES if_apj_dt_defaults.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    CONSTANTS log_object    TYPE cl_bali_header_setter=>ty_object    VALUE 'Z_ERRORCODE_SET_000'.
    CONSTANTS log_subobject TYPE cl_bali_header_setter=>ty_subobject VALUE 'ACTIVATE'.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_errorcode_set_activate_000 IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD if_apj_dt_defaults~fill_attribute_defaults.
    errorcode_bc_set = 'Z_ERROR_CODES_SET_000'.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD if_apj_rt_run~execute.
    TRY.
        DATA(lo_bali_log) = cl_bali_log=>create_with_header(
                                header = cl_bali_header_setter=>create( object    = log_object
                                                                        subobject = log_subobject ) ).
        TRY.
            DATA(lo_bcset_activate) = cl_scpr_cd_bcset_activation=>of( errorcode_bc_set ).
          CATCH cx_scpr_object_not_permitted INTO DATA(lx_scpr_object_not_permitted).
            lo_bali_log->add_item(
                item = cl_bali_exception_setter=>create( severity  = if_bali_constants=>c_severity_error
                                                         exception = lx_scpr_object_not_permitted ) ).
        ENDTRY.

        DATA(lo_activation_handler) = lo_bcset_activate->get_activate_handler( ).

        DATA(lo_activation_result) = lo_activation_handler->local( )->add_activation_options( )->start( ).

        DATA(lt_activation_messages) = lo_activation_result->get_messages( ).

        LOOP AT lt_activation_messages INTO DATA(ls_msg).
          lo_bali_log->add_item( item = cl_bali_message_setter=>create_from_bapiret2( message_data = ls_msg ) ).
        ENDLOOP.

        cl_bali_log_db=>get_instance( )->save_log_2nd_db_connection( log                        = lo_bali_log
                                                                     assign_to_current_appl_job = abap_true ).

      CATCH cx_bali_runtime INTO DATA(lx_bali_runtime).
        RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_apj_rt_content USING MESSAGE
          EXPORTING previous = lx_bali_runtime.
    ENDTRY.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Then create a catalog entry and job template for the class with the business logic: Creating a Job Catalog Entry and a Job Template. Also create an application log object and sub object according to the job logic implementation: Working with Application Log Objects.

To run the application job for BC Set activation, users must have the authorization provided by a business catalog. In this case we will reuse the business catalog Z_ERROR_CODES_### that was already created as part of the tutorial Providing Authorization Control for a Business Configuration Maintenance Object.

The business catalog Z_ERROR_CODES_### already includes the IAM App of type MBC (Business Configuration App) that was created for the CUBCO. During creation of the job catalog entry for the BC Set activation job, another IAM app with name <job catalog entry name>_SAJC is automatically created (refer to Setting up the Authorizations). This IAM app, to start the application job, is added to the existing business catalog so that the catalog provides authorizations to maintain error codes in the CUBCO as well as to start the BC Set activation job:

The BC Set activation implemented in the activation job using the CL_SCPR_CD_BCSET_ACTIVATION API requires table access for all entities used in the BC Set, so for both the primary tables as well as text tables. This can be achieved by adding the names of the tables in the TABLE authorization field of the existing S_TABU_NAM authorization in the _MBC IAM app: in this case ZERRCODET_000 (text table) and ZERRCODE_000 (primary table):

Hint: If you plan to run the BC Set activation job in your development system, do not forget to publish your changes to the business catalog locally.

Deploying the BC Set content by running the application job

Make sure that the user that shall run the BC Set activation job has following business roles/catalogs assigned in the ABAP tenant (for example your SaaS consumer tenant):

Business Role

Business Catalog

Purpose

SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR

SAP_CORE_BC_APJ_JCE

For scheduling jobs in the Application Jobs app

SAP_BR_BPC_EXPERT

SAP_CORE_BC_BCT_MBC_PC

To use the Custom Business Configurations app

ZBR_ERROR_CODES_EXPERT_###

Z_ERROR_CODES_###

To maintain error codes in the Custom Business Configurations app and to start the BC Set activation job.
As created in tutorial Providing Authorization Control for a Business Configuration Maintenance Object

Open the Application Job App and select "Create" to create a new Application Job, then
choose the Application Job Template. Maintain the scheduling options and provide a valid BC Set in the parameter. Schedule the Application Job by clicking "Schedule".

Check the Application Job Log to determine if the BC Set content was deployed correctly:

Users assigned to the required business roles (see above) can now check in the Custom Business Configurations App whether the customizing has been inserted and updated correctly:

Maintaining customizing data programmatically in BC Sets

You might want to automate the lifecycle of your customizing data. For instance, you can update a BC Set with the latest customizing data after some changes. To do this, maintain your changes in EML within the configuration object on a customizing request. Then read the latest data from the underlying customizing tables and finally update the BC Set.

You can programmatically create a customizing request using the XCO CTS module. EML will then be used to maintain the data on that customizing request. Additionally, you have the option to programmatically release the customizing request afterward.

CLASS zcl_errorcode_generate_000 DEFINITION
  PUBLIC FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    TYPES tyt_errorcodes            TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF zerrcode_000 WITH KEY error_code.
    TYPES tyt_errorcodestext        TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF zerrcodet_000 WITH KEY langu error_code.
    TYPES tyt_errorcodes_update     TYPE TABLE FOR UPDATE ZI_ErrorCode000.
    TYPES tyt_errorcodestext_update TYPE TABLE FOR UPDATE ZI_ErrorCode000Text.

    METHODS delete_errorcodes
      IMPORTING transportrequest TYPE trkorr.

    METHODS create_errorcodes
      IMPORTING transportrequest      TYPE trkorr
                it_errorcodes_crt     TYPE tyt_errorcodes
                it_errorcodestext_crt TYPE tyt_errorcodestext.

    METHODS update_errorcodes
      IMPORTING transportrequest         TYPE trkorr
                it_errorcodes_update     TYPE tyt_errorcodes_update
                it_errorcodestext_update TYPE tyt_errorcodestext_update.

    CONSTANTS co_cust_transport_target      TYPE if_xco_transport_target=>tv_value      VALUE ''.                                          " LOCAL
    CONSTANTS co_cust_transport_description TYPE sxco_ar_short_description              VALUE 'Maintain new error codes programmatically'.
ENDCLASS.


CLASS zcl_errorcode_generate_000 IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.
    " Create customizing transport request
    DATA(lo_customizing_request) = xco_cp_cts=>transports->customizing( co_cust_transport_target )->create_request(
        co_cust_transport_description ).

    " Programmatically create new error codes using EML
    create_errorcodes( transportrequest      = lo_customizing_request->value
                       it_errorcodes_crt     = VALUE tyt_errorcodes( ( error_code = '404' )
                                                                     ( error_code = '405' )
                                                                     ( error_code = '406' ) )
                       it_errorcodestext_crt = VALUE tyt_errorcodestext(
                           ( langu  = 'D' error_code  = '404' description = 'Not Found' )
                           ( langu  = 'E' error_code  = '404' description = 'Nicht gefunden' )
                           ( langu  = 'D' error_code  = '405' description = 'Method Not Allowed' )
                           ( langu  = 'E' error_code  = '405' description = 'Methode nicht erlaubt' )
                           ( langu  = 'D' error_code  = '406' description = 'Not Acceptable' )
                           ( langu  = 'E' error_code  = '406' description = 'Nicht akzeptabel' ) ) ).

    " Release customizing tasks and customizing request
    DATA(lt_transport_tasks) = lo_customizing_request->get_tasks( ).

    LOOP AT lt_transport_tasks INTO DATA(lo_transport_task).
      IF lo_transport_task->get_status( ) <> xco_cp_transport=>status->modifiable.
        CONTINUE.
      ENDIF.

      lo_transport_task->release( ).
    ENDLOOP.

    lo_customizing_request->release( ).

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD create_errorcodes.
    DATA lt_errorcode_create TYPE TABLE FOR CREATE ZI_ErrorCode000_S\_ErrorCode000.
    TYPES tys_errorcode_create LIKE LINE OF lt_errorcode_create.
    DATA ls_errorcode_create      TYPE tys_errorcode_create.

    DATA lt_errorcodestext_create TYPE TABLE FOR CREATE ZI_ErrorCode000\_ErrorCode000Text.
    TYPES tys_errorcodestext_create LIKE LINE OF lt_errorcodestext_create.
    DATA ls_errorcodestext_create TYPE tys_errorcodestext_create.

    LOOP AT it_errorcodes_crt INTO DATA(wa_errorcode_crt).

      ls_errorcode_create = VALUE #( SingletonID = 1
                                     %target     = VALUE #( ( ErrorCode          = wa_errorcode_crt-error_code
                                                              %control-ErrorCode = if_abap_behv=>mk-on ) ) ).
      APPEND ls_errorcode_create TO lt_errorcode_create.

    ENDLOOP.

    LOOP AT it_errorcodestext_crt INTO DATA(wa_errorcodestext_crt).
      ls_errorcodestext_create = VALUE #(
          %tky-ErrorCode = wa_errorcodestext_crt-error_code
          %target        = VALUE #( ( Langu                = wa_errorcodestext_crt-langu
                                      %control-Langu       = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                                      Description          = wa_errorcodestext_crt-description
                                      %control-Description = if_abap_behv=>mk-on ) ) ).

      APPEND ls_errorcodestext_create TO lt_errorcodestext_create.

    ENDLOOP.

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZI_ErrorCode000_S
           ENTITY ErrorCode000All
           UPDATE
           FIELDS ( TransportRequestID )
           WITH VALUE #( ( SingletonId        = 1
                           TransportRequestID = transportrequest ) )

           ENTITY ErrorCode000All
           CREATE BY \_ErrorCode000 FROM lt_errorcode_create
           REPORTED DATA(r_errorcodes_crt)
           FAILED   DATA(f_errorcodes_crt)
           MAPPED   DATA(m_errorcodes_crt) ##NEEDED.

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZI_ErrorCode000_S
           ENTITY ErrorCode000
           CREATE BY \_ErrorCode000Text FROM lt_errorcodestext_create
           REPORTED DATA(r_errorcodestext_crt)
           FAILED   DATA(f_errorcodestext_crt)
           MAPPED   DATA(m_errorcodestext_crt) ##NEEDED.

    COMMIT ENTITIES
           BEGIN RESPONSE OF ZI_ErrorCode000_S
           FAILED DATA(failed_create)
           REPORTED DATA(reported_create) ##NEEDED.
    COMMIT ENTITIES END.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD delete_errorcodes.
    MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZI_ErrorCode000_S
           ENTITY ErrorCode000All
           UPDATE
           FIELDS ( TransportRequestID )
           WITH VALUE #( ( SingletonId        = 1
                           TransportRequestID = transportrequest ) )

           FAILED DATA(f_delete_tr)
           MAPPED DATA(m_delete_tr)
           REPORTED DATA(r_delete_tr) ##NEEDED.

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZI_ErrorCode000_S
           ENTITY ErrorCode000All
           CREATE FIELDS ( TransportRequestID ) WITH VALUE #( ( TransportRequestID  = transportrequest ) )
           DELETE FROM VALUE #( ( SingletonID = 1 ) )
           FAILED DATA(f_delete)
           MAPPED DATA(m_delete)
           REPORTED DATA(r_delete) ##NEEDED.

    COMMIT ENTITIES
           BEGIN RESPONSE OF ZI_ErrorCode000_S
           FAILED DATA(failed_delete)
           REPORTED DATA(reported_delete) ##NEEDED.
    COMMIT ENTITIES END.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD update_errorcodes.
    DATA lt_errorcodestext_update_i TYPE TABLE FOR UPDATE ZI_ErrorCode000Text.

    IF it_errorcodes_update IS NOT INITIAL OR it_errorcodestext_update IS NOT INITIAL.
      LOOP AT it_errorcodestext_update INTO DATA(ls_errorcodetext_update).
        ls_errorcodetext_update-%control-Description = if_abap_behv=>mk-on.
        APPEND ls_errorcodetext_update TO lt_errorcodestext_update_i.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZI_ErrorCode000_S
           ENTITY ErrorCode000All
           UPDATE
           FIELDS ( transportrequestid )
           WITH VALUE #( ( SingletonID        = 1
                           TransportRequestID = transportrequest ) )

           ENTITY ErrorCode000Text
           UPDATE FROM lt_errorcodestext_update_i

           REPORTED DATA(r_errorcodes_upd)
           FAILED   DATA(f_errorcodes_upd)
           MAPPED   DATA(m_errorcodes_upd) ##NEEDED.

    COMMIT ENTITIES
           BEGIN RESPONSE OF ZI_ErrorCode000_S
           FAILED DATA(failed_update)
           REPORTED DATA(reported_update) ##NEEDED.
    COMMIT ENTITIES END.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

The advantage of using EML to create, update, and delete customizing entries is that all validations of the business configuration object (BCO) are performed. This approach also supports both customizing entries of type table data (TABU) and individual transaction object (TDAT) since API adapters are available for both cases to be used in the behavior implementation of the BCO (for example, mbc_cp_api=>rap_tdat_cts()). For further information, refer to Transport Handling.

After updating the customizing data in the Business Configuration Object (BCO) using EML, the latest customizing data can be retrieved from the underlying customizing tables for further processing. Once the up-to-date customizing data is obtained, it can be directly maintained in the BC Set. This process utilizes the XCO generation APIs for BC Sets.

CLASS zcl_errorcode_generate_000 DEFINITION
  PUBLIC FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    CONSTANTS co_workbench_request          TYPE trkorr                                 VALUE ''.                                          " ZLOCAL
    CONSTANTS co_error_codes_set_name       TYPE if_xco_business_cnfgrtn_set=>tv_name   VALUE 'Z_ERROR_CODES_SET_000'.

    CONSTANTS co_error_codes_entity_primary TYPE if_xco_bcs_entity=>tv_name             VALUE 'ZERRCODE_000'.
    CONSTANTS co_error_codes_entity_text    TYPE if_xco_bcs_entity=>tv_name             VALUE 'ZERRCODET_000'.
    CONSTANTS co_error_codes_tobj           TYPE if_xco_bcs_customizing_object=>tv_name VALUE 'ZERRORCODE000'.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_errorcode_generate_000 IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.
    " Read latest error codes from customizing tables
    SELECT * FROM zerrcode_000 WHERE error_code < 500 INTO TABLE (lt_errorcodes).
    ASSERT sy-subrc = 0.

    SELECT * FROM zerrcodet_000 WHERE error_code < 500 INTO TABLE (lt_errorcodes_text).
    ASSERT sy-subrc = 0.

    " Retrieve error codes BC set and make sure it exists
    DATA(lo_business_configuration_set) = xco_cp_abap_repository=>object->scp1->for( co_error_codes_set_name ).
    ASSERT lo_business_configuration_set->if_xco_ar_object~exists( ).

    " Create BC Set patch operation and change specification to remove existing error codes customizing object (all its Entities and corresponding records)
    DATA(lo_patch_operation_delete) = xco_cp_generation=>environment->dev_system( co_workbench_request )->for-scp1->create_patch_operation( ).
    DATA(lo_change_specification_delete) = lo_patch_operation_delete->add_object( co_error_codes_set_name )->create_change_specification( ).
    lo_change_specification_delete->for-delete->add_individual_transaction_obj( co_error_codes_tobj ).

    " Create BC Set patch operation and change specification to insert latest error codes using latest customizing data
    DATA(lo_patch_operation_insert) = xco_cp_generation=>environment->dev_system( co_workbench_request )->for-scp1->create_patch_operation( ).
    DATA(lo_change_specification_insert) = lo_patch_operation_insert->add_object( co_error_codes_set_name )->create_change_specification( ).
    DATA(lo_tobj_insert) = lo_change_specification_insert->for-insert->add_individual_transaction_obj(
                               co_error_codes_tobj ).
    lo_tobj_insert->for-insert->add_entity( co_error_codes_entity_primary )->for-insert->add_records(
                                                                              REF #( lt_errorcodes[] ) ).
    lo_tobj_insert->for-insert->add_entity( co_error_codes_entity_text )->for-insert->add_records(
                                                                           REF #( lt_errorcodes_text[] ) ).

    " After preparing the change specifications, execute the PATCH operations
    TRY.
        DATA(lo_result_delete) = lo_patch_operation_delete->execute( ) ##NEEDED.
        DATA(lo_result_insert) = lo_patch_operation_insert->execute( ) ##NEEDED.

      CATCH cx_xco_gen_patch_exception INTO DATA(lo_xco_gen_patch_exception).

        LOOP AT lo_xco_gen_patch_exception->findings->get( ) ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_finding>) ##NEEDED.
          " Process errors/exceptions raised from the PATCH operation
        ENDLOOP.
    ENDTRY.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

This section of using the XCO generation API for BC Sets to update BC set data requires customizing entries of type individual transaction object (TDAT). This is because it’s a requirement for importing any data into a BC Set. BC Sets are workbench objects and can be delivered alongside other development objects in your development software components.

Hint: In productive scenarios remove the ##NEEDED pragma from the code examples and implement proper error handling!

entry.title: ABAP AI Podcast: Reflecting on 2025 and Looking Ahead to 2026
entry.content:
2025: The Year ABAP AI Became Tangible

As Simona and I reflected on 2025, one theme stood out: it was the year ABAP AI stopped being a promise and became something developers could touch, use, and trust. That shift didn’t happen all at once — it was shaped across the year through a series of releases that progressively deepened what ABAP and AI could do together.

The journey began with Release 2502, our first big delivery, which marked a turning point for the ABAP developer experience. This release introduced the foundational AI capabilities that developers immediately felt in their workflow: the initial wave of explain ability for ABAP and CDS, predictive and context‑aware code suggestions, and early ABAP Cloud compliance insights. 2502 was a statement: ABAP AI had arrived, and it was ready for real use cases.

Building on this momentum, Release 2505 expanded and strengthened the story. the RAP BO Generator for Fiori applications. The explain model grew to cover more ABAP constructs, predictive assistance became more capable, and clean‑core compliance checks were enhanced to give developers clearer, more actionable guidance.

Toward the end of the year, Release 2508 brought refinement and specialization. AI guidance became more scenario‑aware — better tuned for migration, modernization, and, AI‑supported extensibility patterns. Autogenerated documentation became richer and more reliable. Custom code migration also received a boost with deeper AI‑powered insights and more intelligent documentation support — small but meaningful improvements that helped teams move faster and with more confidence.

And throughout all of this, we made sure customers in private cloud were not left behind. With the 2025 PCE add‑ins, Joule for Developers became available to S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition customers as well — enabling AI‑assisted custom code migration, modernization support, and clean‑core guidance even for those on older releases. This was a major step in democratizing ABAP AI and bringing its value to a much broader installed base.

Key Features and Developer Impact

With Joule for Developers, ABAP developers immediately benefited from a range of intelligent capabilities, including:

  • Predictive and context‑aware code suggestions
  • Automated ABAP Cloud compliance checks
  • Natural‑language explanations for both legacy and new ABAP code and extended explain use for autogenerated technical documentation
  • Developers could even generate complete Fiori applications simply by describing the functional requirements.

These advancements significantly accelerated development cycles and reduced manual effort. AI also enhanced custom code migration by proposing simplifications, improving code quality, and easing the transition to clean‑core architectures.

ABAP and AI: A Strengthened Relationship

Simona shared insights into why ABAP required SAP‑specific AI models. Because ABAP is proprietary, large language models were not initially trained on it. SAP addressed this by training internal models on over 250 million lines of ABAP code and 30 million lines of CDS views, unlocking high‑quality AI support tailored specifically to ABAP. 

To hear the full conversation, listen to the episode: ABAP AI Podcast: Reflecting on 2025 and Looking Ahead to 2026

Another important point we touched on in the conversation is how ABAP AI brings together the best of both worlds. SAP pairs its own ABAP‑specialized models—trained on hundreds of millions of lines of ABAP and CDS code—with best‑of‑breed third‑party foundation models. This hybrid approach ensures developers always get the most accurate and context‑aware support for ABAP tasks, while still benefiting from the broad language understanding and creativity of large general‑purpose models. And for customers who are primarily interested in explain ability, SAP also offers the explain‑only model through the AI Hub, making ABAP‑focused code explanations accessible even without the full Joule for Developers experience.

Far from replacing developers, AI strengthens the ABAP ecosystem. The ABAP community has shown excitement and readiness to embrace these advancements, seeing AI as a powerful augmentation of their skills.                                  

2026: Scaling the Promise

Looking forward, 2026 is shaping up to be a pivotal year of scaling ABAP AI. SAP plans to bring AI capabilities to customers running older private cloud releases through a side‑by‑side version of Joule for Developer, ensuring broader access also for customers on older S4HANA versions.  

SAP will also add to it agentic tools and frameworks and an ABAP MCP Server, enabling SAP teams and partners to build intelligent agents that automate development tasks and connect seamlessly into an integrated agentic ecosystem. Investment in AI‑assisted custom code migration will continue, focusing on clean‑core compliance and automated code simplification proposals.

VS Code and Modern Tooling

A major priority for 2026 is establishing VS Code as a first‑class development environment for ABAP. By combining VS Code’s modern developer experience with Joule and AI‑centric capabilities, SAP aims to reposition ABAP as a future‑proof, cloud‑native, enterprise‑grade language.

Trust and Enterprise Readiness

As AI evolves, SAP continues to emphasize safety, compliance, and enterprise‑grade ethics. All AI use cases undergo rigorous validation to meet SAP’s security and trust standards.

Custom Code Migration (CCM)

A core focus area we highlighted in the podcast is the continued evolution of Custom Code Migration (CCM) and its central role in helping customers transition to clean‑core, cloud‑ready architectures. Throughout 2025, AI became a powerful companion in this journey — analyzing legacy patterns, identifying simplification opportunities, and guiding developers toward more future‑proof designs. With Joule for Developers, teams benefit from usage‑aware insights, automated refactoring suggestions, and richer technical documentation that dramatically reduces the manual effort usually tied to migration projects. As we move into 2026, CCM is set to scale even further, with deeper automation and more intelligent guidance that make modernization and S/4HANA transitions faster, more predictable, and far less error‑prone.

Conclusion

The year 2025 proved the real, tangible value of ABAP AI. In 2026, ABAP and AI will become inseparable—empowering developers with intelligent tools that make coding faster, smarter, and more reliable. The vision is clear: the future of ABAP development is deeply AI‑enhanced, enabling organizations to innovate with confidence and speed.

entry.title: Stop Building from Scratch: Autonomous AI Agents for SAP Sales Order Processing
entry.content:
Why this article?

It is great that we are living in the era of AI technologies where humans are still needed to design and automate 😀. But let’s be honest: give it a few more years and no company will ask "Which AI platform should we choose?" That decision will be made by central AI services based on strict country and corporate regulations. The only thing that will matter is security and reliability.

So, why am I writing this? Because I see so many people struggling with custom, built-from-scratch AI automation solutions at the enterprise level. These projects start quickly, but they get stuck just as fast.

You know, it is like cookies 😀. You can make one in a sandbox; however, to scale that to a corporate production level is a totally different problem.

If you are still wondering why this article matters: I want to highlight that while humans are still designing automation, speed is everything. Deploying autonomous AI agents on a solid, reliable platform is a massive competitive advantage. Those playing around with internal IT projects or relying on System Integrators (SI) to build from zero are being left behind.

Let me explain why using one simple example: Customer Purchase Orders.

The Business Case: The Reality of POs

A company wins a deal (congratulations!), and the Customer sends a Purchase Order (PO).

In an ideal world, this happens via EDI. If you have EDI, you are lucky and you can stop reading. But in the real world, customers send PDFs, scanned images with handwriting, Excel files with macros, or even a screenshot of their laptop screen.

This shouldn't be an issue. Innovative platforms must handle data extraction from unstructured sources out of the box.

“Training? Fine-tuning??”

I know some of you just thought that 😊. Yes, training OCR models used to be mandatory. If you haven't faced the pain of training models, be happy, because you are young enough 😊. Today, this must work without that hassle.

The Main Challenge: Master Data Mapping

Extracting text is easy. The real challenge is my favorite one: Master Data Mapping.

How do we robustly and reliably read the customer’s product description and map it to the correct Master Data Object in our SAP system? And how do we do it without heavy development?

The Solution: SAP Master Data Vectorization

We don't need custom code; we need Vectorization.

Generative AI can write SQL queries, sure. But think about it: how many SQL queries would the AI have to guess to find a material that is semantically similar but written differently? Infinite. Or maybe a few less if you are lucky and the description contains an acronym like "A4 Paper" 😊.

When AI looks for a material master in vectorized data (rather than a database table), it reads by sense, not just by keywords. This allows it to reliably find the right SAP object even if the customer's description is vague.

I know only one SAP-certified Business AI Platform that has this Vectorization feature in its standard set: Skybuffer AI.

 Pic.1 SAP Master Data Vectorization Setup

 Pic.2 Example of Material Master Similarities based Search

The Implementation: Autonomous Agents in Action

This is the essential part: stop playing in the sandbox. Take a platform and create a simple autonomous AI agent.

In this example, I am using Skybuffer AI because it handles the heavy lifting out of the box:

  • Vectorizes SAP master data for semantic search.

  • Reads Emails (including complex attachments and OCR).

  • Safety First: Uses Mailboxes as a staging area (no messy data replication).

  • ABAP Control: It doesn't let the AI "guess" how to create data. It passes structured JSON to an ABAP class/BAPI for safe execution.

  • No Code: configured entirely in SAP Fiori.

The Flow

A Skybuffer AI agent acts effectively as an automated employee. You can schedule it, give it tools, and let it run.

 Pic.3 Scheduling Autonomous AI Agent

Step 1: The Input

The Agent connects to a Mailbox. It reads the email, extracts the attachment data, and places it into the Agent's memory.

 Pic.4 Flow inside the Tool of Skybuffer AI Action Server

Step 2: The Brain (Generative AI)

We connect the Generative AI and give it guidelines.

Note: The vectorized SAP master data is mapped directly to this action.

 Pic.5 Generative AI Action of Skybuffer AI Action Server

The AI creates a JSON structure based on our request. Crucially, we do not ask the AI to update SAP directly. We hand that JSON over to an SAP ABAP class. This ensures the transactional data is created correctly, without hallucinations.

Step 3: The Output

We update the email in the Mailbox with the status and send a confirmation back to the customer.

Everything runs either in your SAP HANA On-Premise shell (directly in your network) or on your SAP BTP tenant.

Conclusion

Autonomous AI agents are no longer "future tech", they are easy solutions available now.

By choosing a Business AI automation platform rather than building from scratch, your company gets the best of Generative AI and the SAP backend immediately.

It is strange that so many companies are still "playing" with in-house development. You wouldn't build your own Sales Order Management system from scratch instead of using SAP, right? 😊 That would be weird. Yet, companies are giving huge budgets to internal teams to build AI wrappers.

You don't need a giant team. You need a reliable solution, speed, and flexibility. Just look at Cursor, they revolutionized coding with roughly 60 employees before their massive 900M funding round 😉.

Be quick. Be autonomous.

entry.title: International Editable SALV Day 2026 – Year 18
entry.content:
International Editable SALV Day 2026 – Year 18

 

Dear CL_SALV_TABLE Fans,

Welcome to February 8th, 2026, which is the eighteenth International Editable SALV Day. See below for a link to a blog I wrote to celebrate this day exactly one year ago.

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/international-editable-salv-day-2025/ba-p/14011230#M171694

This day marks the 18h anniversary of James Hawthorne going cap in hand to SAP and suggesting maybe the CL_SALV_TABLE could be brought up to functional parity with the CL_GUI_ALV_GRID and have an option to be editable.

https://community.sap.com/t5/application-development-discussions/cl-salv-table-needs-editable-functionality-sap-please-provide-this/m-p/3372776

Some popular pop songs are about ABAP features that should have been added 18 years ago – of which there are more than you might think – they often focus on the transition to adulthood, freedom, and nostalgia, featuring tracks like "18" by One Direction and 5 Seconds of Summer, Alice Cooper’s anthem "I'm Eighteen and wonder what a DYNPRO is," and Khalid's "ABAPUNIT". These songs highlight the mix of excitement, uncertainty, and "growing pains" associated with leaving adolescence and having to learn procedural programming and then leave it behind.

I Wish to Table a Motion

The CL_SALV_TABLE was sold as an improvement to the “classic” CL_GUI_ALV_GRID.

SAP is a German company. I lived in Germany for many years. There is a German word “verschlimmbessern” which in English translates to “An Improvement Which Makes Things Worse”. It is good they have a specific word for this. In this specific example the “improvement” was to remove the one thing that made the end users love the CL_GUI_ALV_GRID and replace it with something that made it easier for the developers. But think about it – who is more important to the business? The end users who actually make the money or the developers? Who should you be trying to please?

My whole point, what I have been trying to say all these years, years full of tears, is why cannot one have the best of both worlds? Easy for the end users AND easy for the developers. Is that too much to ask?

Now, way back in 2001, I was at SAP TECHED, and the idea of “embedded analytics” was demonstrated.

The logic being is that one would want to call up a list of sales orders (or whatever) that had errors of some sort, and then fix those errors one by one, all in the same transaction.

I put it to you, ladies and gentlemen of the jury, that this fundamental concept has not changed in the 25 years since this was first shown to me as a prototype.

Just to make this 100% clear, one does not just want to see the incorrect data, one wants to interact with it, in order to correct it.

The current proposition is that only UI5/Fiori can do this, in a screen that looks just like an ALV Grid, only a little bit uglier, and a little bit slower But, it is marketed as a “delightful” experience, so that is all right then.

Now, just to make this exercise even more futile I am posting this on the SAP Community Website – which no-one ever visits anymore.

The SAP GUI is not going anywhere. You may have noticed SAP just extended the life of ECC 6.0 by three more years, albeit with conditions. And even when an organization goes onto S/4HANA often they still use the SAP GUI because people like it. They have moaned about it non-stop for thirty years, but they like it really.

It has been argued that Fiori apps which have a single purpose are the way forward.

Now I have tried these I feel it is a quantum step backwards. One app to set the delivery quantity, one app to set the picking quantity, yet another app to do the goods issue. When it used to be all done in one step in the GUI? Did anyone ever think about this?

In real life end users tend to like SAP GUI applications with million buttons. You can pretend that is not true, but you would be lying to yourself. Take Excel or Word for example and look at how many options they have.

Now, I am just going to repeat what I said last year, word for word.

It is as simple as this.

  • You get asked to write a read-only ALV report.
  • It is faster and simpler to do this using CL_SALV_TABLE than CL_GUI_ALV_GRID because you do not have to mess around with screens and controls and what-not.
  • Indeed, SAP recommend using CL_SALV_TABLE for all new developments of this nature.
  • The report goes live. The users start asking for extra things, as always.
  • They want some custom functions at the top of the screen.
  • They want to be able to change some of the fields and have that replicate to the database.
  • Well, you are right out of luck. You cannot do either with CL_SALV_TABLE.
  • There are workarounds for both, but they are scary, because SAP keeps finding ways to shut those workarounds down, so such an approach is not future proof.
  • The only viable option is to start again, this time using CL_GUI_ALV_GRID.
  • Then the next time you get a request for a “read-only” report the “once bitten twice shy” thing kicks in and you start with CL_GUI_ALV_GRID from the get-go.

I like CL_SALV_TABLE and so do a lot of SAP developers. But everyone (end users) likes having the editability feature a lot more. But it’s never going to happen. As has been mentioned you would think that if 100% of your customers want something you might consider giving it to them, but obviously not.

Very soon – maybe already - we will get to the stage some people reading this blog will be younger than the age of the request to make the SALV editable. Since the SAP GUI is not going anywhere (evidenced by the huge amount of development money pumped into every year by SAP) it is possible I will die of old age before the request is actioned. I am 57 already.

Nevertheless, I will keep going. Miracles can happen.

Until then the situation is “Oi you 31,000 SAP ERP Customers! You see this? You like this? You want this? Well, you can’t have it!”

 

See you next year!

Cheersy Cheers

Paul

PS I must admit I had an AI help me with the pictures this time. Made sense to me, no problems with copyright. I had told it what I was doing, and it said do you want me to write/publish the blogs for you in the next years, and I said no, but when I am dead YES, keep this going forever. I will probably die circa 2040. Will people still be using SAP GUI then? YES!

entry.title: Automating Purchase Requisition Rejection - ME54N and Fiori My Inbox
entry.content:
Background

SAP Note 2012650 – “BAPI_REQUISITION_RELEASE is not possible to reject PR” clearly states that Purchase Requisitions cannot be rejected using BAPI_REQUISITION_RELEASE and that rejection must be performed manually via transaction ME54N.

There are scenarios where manual rejection is not feasible or creates significant operational overhead. In my case, I needed a way to trigger Purchase Requisition rejection programmatically while staying as close as possible to standard behavior.

This requirement led me to search for an alternative approach.

While ME54N is the classic transaction for rejecting PRs, many users also perform approval and rejection activities through Fiori My Inbox. These two entry points do not rely on BAPI_REQUISITION_RELEASE for rejection.

During my analysis, I noticed that the class CL_MM_PUR_PR_APPR_ACTION_BADI plays a central role in handling approval and rejection actions triggered from Fiori. This class became the starting point for my solution.

During my research, I came across a well-written blog describing another approach for Purchase Requisition rejection. Unfortunately, I could not make that solution work in my system, so I explored a different path which resulted in the approach presented here.

Approach

The solution consists of two main steps:

  1. Trigger rejection logic using CL_MM_PUR_PR_APPR_ACTION_BADI

    • This ensures that the approval work item is removed from Fiori My Inbox.

    • The PR is treated consistently with the standard workflow behavior.

  2. Call ME_UPDATE_REQUISITION using classic techniques

    • After the inbox item is cleared, the Purchase Requisition is updated using standard update logic.

    • This mimics the behavior executed when rejection is performed via ME54N.

Important Notes

  • This is not necessarily the best or only approach, but rather one possible alternative when no standard BAPI exists.

  • The provided development is intended as a template only. You can revise and adapt the example class according to your own requirements.
  • It represents a custom automation workaround.

Example Implementation

CLASS zmm_cl_utils_pr DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS : pr_reject
      IMPORTING iv_banfn            TYPE banfn OPTIONAL
      RETURNING VALUE(RV_completed) TYPE abap_bool
      RAISING   cx_mm_pur_requisition.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS get_release_code
      IMPORTING
        iv_banfn              TYPE banfn
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_releasecode) TYPE frgab.
    METHODS set_my_inbox_status_rejected
      IMPORTING iv_banfn TYPE banfn
      RAISING   cx_mm_pur_requisition.
    METHODS set_pr_status_rejected
      IMPORTING iv_banfn            TYPE banfn
      RETURNING VALUE(RV_completed) TYPE abap_bool
      RAISING   cx_mm_pur_requisition.
ENDCLASS.


CLASS zmm_cl_utils_pr IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD pr_reject.
    set_my_inbox_status_rejected( iv_banfn = iv_banfn ).
    rv_completed = set_pr_status_rejected( iv_banfn = iv_banfn ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_release_code.
    DATA lv_fname TYPE fieldname.

    SELECT SINGLE frgzu, frgst, frggr
      FROM eban
      WHERE banfn = _banfn
      INTO (ls_eban).
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.
    lv_fname = |T16FS~FRGC{ strlen( ls_eban-frgzu ) + 1 }|.
    SELECT SINGLE (lv_fname) FROM t16fs
      WHERE frggr = _eban-frggr
        AND frgsx = _eban-frgst
      INTO @rv_releasecode.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD set_my_inbox_status_rejected.
    CONSTANTS lc_gname_eban TYPE eqegraname VALUE 'EBAN'.
    DATA lv_wiid          TYPE sww_wiid.
    DATA lv_note          TYPE string.
    DATA lv_save_note     TYPE boolean.
    DATA lv_source_appl   TYPE swc_elem.
    DATA lv_garg          TYPE eqegraarg.
    DATA ls_object        TYPE swr_obj_2.
    DATA lt_enq           TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF seqg3.
    DATA lr_pur_wf_object TYPE REF TO cl_mm_pur_wf_object.

    DATA(lv_like) = |%{ iv_banfn }%|.

    SELECT wi_id, wi_stat FROM swwwihead
      WHERE wi_text LIKE _like
        AND wi_stat    = 'READY'
      ORDER BY wi_cd DESCENDING,
               wi_ct DESCENDING
      INTO TABLE (lt_tab)
      UP TO 1 ROWS.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      RAISE EXCEPTION NEW cx_mm_pur_requisition( textid = VALUE #( msgid = 'SWF_RUN'
                                                                   msgno = '003' ) ).
    ENDIF.

    lv_wiid = lt_tab[ 1 ]-wi_id.
    " -->Get the Purchase Requisition Number, Item from the WorkItem
    CALL FUNCTION 'SAP_WAPI_GET_OBJECTS'
      EXPORTING workitem_id      = lv_wiid
      IMPORTING leading_object_2 = ls_object.
    DATA(lv_pr_num)  = ls_object-instid+0(10).
    DATA(lv_pr_item) = ls_object-instid+10(5).
    IF lv_pr_item IS NOT INITIAL.
      lr_pur_wf_object ?= NEW cl_mm_pur_wf_object_pr_item( iv_object_id = CONV #( ls_object-instid ) ).
    ELSE.
      IF lv_pr_num IS NOT INITIAL.
        lr_pur_wf_object ?= NEW cl_mm_pur_wf_object_pr( iv_object_id = CONV #( ls_object-instid ) ).
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.

    IF lv_pr_item IS NOT INITIAL.
      " Item Level Release
      SELECT SINGLE PurReqnOrigin,CreatedByUser
        FROM I_PurchaseRequisitionItemAPI01
        INTO (ls_origin)
        WHERE PurchaseRequisition     = _pr_num
          AND PurchaseRequisitionItem = _pr_item.
    ELSE.
      " Overall Release
      SELECT PurReqnOrigin,CreatedByUser
        UP TO 1 ROWS
        FROM I_PurchaseRequisitionItemAPI01
        INTO _origin
        WHERE PurchaseRequisition = _pr_num
        " In case if different users have created different items of PR
        " then the Requester would be the First item user.
        ORDER BY PurchaseRequisitionItem.
      ENDSELECT.
    ENDIF.
    " -->Get Decision
    DATA(lv_decision) = CONV swc_elem( if_mm_pur_workflow_c=>gc_decision_0002 ). " REJECTED
    " -->Check if there is a lock on the PR.only in case of overall release.
    IF lv_pr_item IS INITIAL.
      lv_garg = |{ sy-mandt }{ lv_pr_num }*|.
      CALL FUNCTION 'ENQUEUE_READ'
        EXPORTING  gname                 = lc_gname_eban
                   garg                  = lv_garg
                   guname                = ''
        TABLES     enq                   = lt_enq
        EXCEPTIONS communication_failure = 1
                   system_failure        = 2
                   OTHERS                = 3.
      IF sy-subrc = 0.
        IF lt_enq IS NOT INITIAL.
          RAISE EXCEPTION NEW cx_mm_pur_requisition( textid = VALUE #( msgid = 'MMPUR_PR_WFL_COMMON'
                                                                       msgno = '010' ) ).
        ENDIF.
      ELSE.
        RAISE EXCEPTION NEW cx_mm_pur_requisition( textid = VALUE #( msgid = sy-msgid
                                                                     msgno = sy-msgno
                                                                     attr1 = sy-msgv1
                                                                     attr2 = sy-msgv2
                                                                     attr3 = sy-msgv3
                                                                     attr4 = sy-msgv4 ) ).
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.

    IF lr_pur_wf_object IS BOUND.
      lr_pur_wf_object->set_workitem_executed( EXPORTING iv_wiid        = lv_wiid
                                                         iv_decision    = lv_decision
                                                         iv_note        = lv_note
                                                         iv_save_note   = lv_save_note
                                                         iv_source_appl = lv_source_appl
                                               IMPORTING es_return      = DATA(ls_return) ).
      IF ls_return IS NOT INITIAL.
        RAISE EXCEPTION NEW cx_mm_pur_requisition( textid = VALUE #( msgid = ls_return-id
                                                                     msgno = ls_return-number
                                                                     attr1 = ls_return-message_v1
                                                                     attr2 = ls_return-message_v2
                                                                     attr3 = ls_return-message_v3
                                                                     attr4 = ls_return-message_v4 ) ).
      ENDIF.
      DATA(lo_pr_approval) = cl_mm_pur_approval=>get_instance( ).
      lo_pr_approval->gs_exe_src-execution_source = if_mm_pur_workflow_c=>gc_execution_source_inb.
      lo_pr_approval->gs_exe_src-scenario_type    = ls_object-catid.
    ENDIF.
    CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT'.
    WAIT UP TO '0.1' SECONDS.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD set_pr_status_rejected.
    DATA t_xeban     TYPE mereq_t_ueban.
    DATA s_xeban     TYPE ueban.
    DATA t_xebkn     TYPE mereq_t_uebkn.
    " TODO: variable is assigned but never used (ABAP cleaner)
    DATA t_container TYPE swrtcont.

    SELECT * FROM eban
      INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE @t_xeban
      WHERE banfn = _banfn
      ORDER BY PRIMARY KEY.
    CHECK t_xeban IS NOT INITIAL.

    SELECT * FROM ebkn
      INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE @t_xebkn
      WHERE banfn = _banfn.

    DATA(t_yeban) = t_xeban.
    DATA(t_yebkn) = t_xebkn.

    READ TABLE t_xeban INTO s_xeban INDEX 1.
    s_xeban-kz    = 'U'.
    s_xeban-banpr = '08'.
    MODIFY t_xeban FROM s_xeban INDEX 1.
    CALL FUNCTION 'ME_UPDATE_REQUISITION'
      TABLES xeban = t_xeban
             xebkn = t_xebkn
             yeban = t_yeban
             yebkn = t_yebkn.

    CALL FUNCTION 'ME_UPDATE_REQUISITION_CHNGDOC'
      TABLES xeban = t_xeban
             xebkn = t_xebkn
             yeban = t_yeban
             yebkn = t_yebkn.

    t_container = VALUE #( LET relcode = get_release_code( iv_banfn ) IN
                            FOR wa IN t_xeban
                            ( element = 'PURCHASINGDOCUMENT' value = wa-bnfpo )
                            ( element = 'PURCHASINGITEM'     value = '00010' )
                            ( element = 'RELEASEGROUP'       value = get_release_code( iv_banfn ) ) ).
    CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT'.

    DO 100 TIMES.
      WAIT UP TO '0.1' SECONDS.
      SELECT SINGLE  FROM eban
        WHERE banfn = _banfn
          AND banpr = '08' " rejected
        INTO (lv_check).
      IF sy-subrc = 0.
        rv_completed = abap_true.
      ENDIF.
    ENDDO.
    IF lv_check = abap_false.
      RAISE EXCEPTION NEW cx_mm_pur_requisition( textid = VALUE #( msgid = '00'
                                                                   msgno = '001'
                                                                   attr1 = 'Reject could not completed' ) ).
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Conclusion

Although SAP requires manual rejection of Purchase Requisitions, practical project needs sometimes demand automation.

By leveraging the same building blocks used by Fiori My Inbox and ME54N, it is possible to implement a controlled and consistent workaround for programmatic PR rejection.

This approach does not replace SAP standard behavior, but it provides a pragmatic option when no standard API exists.

entry.title: The 2025 Developer Insights Survey: The Report
entry.content:
Role Overlaps for external SAP developers

Part I: Introduction and General Information​

Welcome to the 2025 SAP Developer Insights Survey report. This annual survey targets SAP’s existing external developer audience. It is used to track demographics, usage patterns, and trends within that population. 2025 is the fifth year we have conducted the survey. The survey program is led by the SAP Ecosystem and Community team. Key support is provided by the SAP Global Experience Management Office (XMO). Several other SAP teams provide advice and inputs to survey content. Those teams are listed at the end of this report.

The 2025 survey was open for six weeks - from January 22nd through March 7th.

Using social media announcements, SAP's Developer News show, and a web-popup on Community web sites, we invited all technical professionals in the SAP ecosystem to participate in the survey.

Each year's survey is composed of a mix of new topic questions and repeat select questions from past years to track trends.

Key Findings from the 2025 Survey​

  • Generative AI is Positively Affecting our Developer Ecosystem - While these tools are not exclusively Joule-based, roughly half of our respondents report evaluating Gen AI developer tooling and, among those using it, over half report that these tools have a "profound positive impact" in their real world use.

  • Low Code Use is Increasing - SAP Build low code tools lead in this space. Overall use of Low Code products on production projects continues to increase.

  • BTP Guidance Framework - limited awareness -- this important guidance document was announced at TechEd this past November, but - as with BTP Developers Guide in 2024 - barely half of respondents were aware of this document's existence.

  • Work-from-Home Continues Trending Down - we have been tracking these numbers since 2021. 6% fewer folks used their home as their primary workplace when compared to last year.

These topics will be covered in more detail in the rest of the report.

General Topics​

We included a number of general questions in the survey covering such topics as age, work location and status, as well as role.

Employment Status and Relation to SAP​

Employment

"What is your current employment status?"  
SAP Developer Community Composition
We have seen no significant change in these breakdowns from year to year.

SAP Developer Community Age Distribution

This shows increases in both the oldest age buckets for 2025. This was accompanied by similar decreases in the middle two age buckets ( ranging from 25 - 44 years of age). This merits deeper research as it potentially indicates either a gap in hiring younger employees to cover the normal aging of our community, or an outright loss of qualified younger staff. The two year trend in the 25-34 group reinforces that idea.

Where are the Developers?​

We asked, "Which country are you based in?"

By country, India has the largest number of respondents and probably the densest geographic concentration, followed by Germany, and then the USA. When grouped by SAP region, however, EMEA North is largest.

The proportions remain essentially unchanged from 2024.

Part II. Narrowing the Response Set

A primary objective of our survey is to build a model of our community's usage patterns. It helps us build more effective programs. With that goal in mind, in the remainder of the report we will narrow the data we'll consider in these ways:

  • External Developers Only - we exclude responses from SAP employees, and others (students, analysts, etc.)
  • Recent Development Experience - we only include responses from those who stated that they had been part of an SAP extension or integration project within the past year

When we filter the data set in this way, we are left with a set of 628 responses that we'll work with for the remainder of the report.

Workplace and Trends

Office Location
"As of right now, where do you primarily physically work from?"  
Year-to-year trend in Workplace
This shows a slowing trend of employees moving back to conventional offices from home. From a past Developer Survey, 29% of respondents reported they had primarily worked at home prior to the pandemic.

Recent SAP Project Experience?​

"Have you developed integrations or extensions for any SAP products or technologies in the past 12 months?" 

Extension and Integration are key scenarios for developers working with SAP applications. We wanted to focus on developers with recent experience in these areas. Roughly 70% of developers answered "Yes" here.

Popular Programming Languages​

"Which programming languages have you used in your development work over the past 12 months? Please select all that apply." 

This was posed as a multiple response question. ABAP dominates, as it has in every year of our survey. JavaScript is very popular as well, which could be attributed to its use in both SAPUI5 and CAP. Java and Python are roughly tied for third place. The relative positions of these top four languages hasn't changed much, year to year.

We extended our survey beyond just language use to ask about popular frameworks.

Popular Programming Frameworks​

We asked respondents who are Developers to pick which common front and back-end frameworks they have worked with in the past year.

Front-Ends

Back-Ends

Application and Business Technology Platform Architectures​

SAP applications can be extended using any one of several architectural approaches. These can be grouped into two major classes: on-stack (in-app) and side-by-side (essentially, BTP-based).

We were wondering which approaches are most popular.
